speaker: interviewer
text: --me on May the 24th, 1995. And this tape number six. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Mr. Gardos, do you have any dreams about your suffering during the Shoah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It happens every now and then. I, It's very rare nowadays. It's been a long time. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It, it comes back, not as a-- not so much as a nightmare, but when, there are many occasions when one can't help but, but think about it and remember it. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Yeah, I don't think that anybody who, who lives through that would, could forget it or, you know, I just, I don't think there's such a human being on earth. Doesn't matter which side of the fence they were, whether it was on the receiving side or, or otherwise.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you spoken to your children when they were older, about your experiences or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have, I have. Not, maybe not so much in detail. I certainly wanted them to know what, what happened. I certainly wanted them to know, to be aware, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not only what happened to me, but also what happened in that, during that time and to our people. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And certainly I-- not for the reason to put, to put fear into them, that god forbid anything like that can ever happen again, but mainly to, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to remember where, where, where they come from and that life wasn't always like it is here now. And I took back my-- not my son but my daughter I took back to the village where I was born. I was there, I showed her things which I think, I think she appreciated and-- I, for some reason, I didn't have an opportunity with my son. But I hope, I'd still like to do it. But whatever it is, I, I want-- the main reason was I wanted to make them aware that ever, if anything, any, ever, any of this sort of ugly danger would threaten our people, not to have the same fate and not to go to the slaughter yard like we did, but to be organized be aware and never let it happen again. Because I don't know, it's easy to say that afterwards. But I just, I just thought many times afterwards, if we would had-- if we would have had the solidarity and the, and the, and the, and the pride and the guts to stand up for ourselves, maybe it would have happened the same to us as what happened in the Warsaw ghetto. The end would have been the same. But at least we wouldn't have, we wouldn't have gone through what, what we did go through.

speaker: interviewer
text: Has your experiences during the war affected how you are observant as a Jew now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I, I grew up in a family and as I, as I said before, an Orthodox family. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I, I, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I must say that my belief and my, my trust has never wavered, not for a minute. I didn't, even when I was lying helpless with the-- with typhus, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I somehow, I firmly believed that I will pull through. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Faith, faith.

speaker: interviewer
text: What Jewish organizations do you belong to now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now? Strange, strangely enough, when you say Jewish organizations, apart from the Holocaust survivors, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there's no, no other Jewish organization I can really name. I mean, what Jewish organization do other people belong to? They belong to the Zionist organizations? Fine. I, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm a strong-- naturally that I'm a supporter of, of Israel. There's no need to say that. But the organizations is-- I have close relationship is the yeshiva. And I'm the president of the South Head Synagogue. So that is maybe and enough for to-- enough to say that organizations-- as comes to organizations, I spend half my time working for, working for organizations which are under the umbrella of the, of the synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you, you believe in the mitzvah of education, as the yeshiva?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely, yeah. I, I believe in any form of a Jewish education. My, my children went to, went to Moriah. Both my children went to Moriah. And I think the only hope of survival as a, as a nation or as Jews is in the future, in the, in the, in their children and in their education. Because if there's no Jewish education, there's, there's no future.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever spoken to any Australian business friends or colleagues about your war experiences? Or has the subject not come up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little, very little. You see, on the whole, people don't, don't like to, don't like to hear, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they don't like to hear it. It might be interesting. But in the beginning-- just for example, when I, when we came to Italy in 1945, the Italians simply didn't believe us, did not believe us-- we were boys of 16-- that we were in concentration camp because we were Jews. And that was especially in the southern part of Italy. They, they didn't know what anti-Semitism was. And they didn't know-- they didn't believe us that people-- that such a thing can be that that people got, got wiped out because they were Jewish. I mean, they were ordinary peasants and simple people. You can't-- we came to Australia. And of course, we come to Australia as, as migrants. And people, are you from Europe, and eventually the war. Oh, it must have been terrible. You know, it wasn't easy here either because, because tea was rationed, and we couldn't get tea, and sort of thing. I mean, when this is people's reaction. And that maybe is natural reaction. There was never any war here on this soil. And please god, there never will be. And, and people just can't understand, can't grasp what it, what, what could, what happened. And if-- I, I noticed it a couple of times when I, when somebody really and truly wanted to know and asked me, a non-Jewish person. And I started to tell what happened, I saw it on the faces, that, the misbelief. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I didn't want to-- I, I just simply, I simply didn't want to talk about it to people because I didn't want them to think that I'm making up stories, eh. And I didn't want to-- I just didn't feel that, that I'm good enough to, to relate it. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when it comes to business people, [SIGHS] that is the last thing what you want to do. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not because of any reason that one is ashamed or one is embarrassed or anything. I don't-- because simply, in, with businesspeople, it doesn't matter how close you, you are to, to a person in business. He only wants to hear the good news.

speaker: interviewer
text: I believe there's something you'd like to add about your family that you thought of after your family appeared on camera.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what was it-- really, what I would like to add is that [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I'm not one of those fathers who don't see their children in, in the real light. And I'm, I'm completely unbiased. I'm, I'm sure I'm unbiased. Because I can see their faults, faults. You-- nobody is, is, is faultless. And nobody is infallible. But I'm really-- that's where, that's where I think I have achieved. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When I look at my children. [SIGHS] And, and I'm really, really so proud of them, to what they are. Not, not only what, what they make, what they make, what they make out of life financially, but what, what, what persons they are, the person themselves. That is, that is what what [INAUDIBLE] me. And with all the, with all the faults they have, I think they're, they're just wonderful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just by way of closing, what was the main reason why you wanted to give your testimony to the Shoah Foundation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what should have been done-- and, and I blame myself as much as anybody else. While there were tens of thousands and hundreds of thousand of ex-servicemen around the world as a whole, and the world, the Jewish, the Jewish organizations, non-Jewish organizations, myself, individuals, we haven't done a thing about it to make sure that it is not going to be said after, after my generation is gone, and there will be no survivors left, that it never happened again. Because all, all, all the people who are claiming that it was a hoax and this has never happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: They wouldn't have, they wouldn't have a leg to stand on if there would have been rallies, if there would have been television proof of, of return soldiers making, making a testimony and testifying in the proper and organized way, people who liberated the camps themselves. I mean, where are, what have we done for 50 years? We were telling stories. We were making films.

speaker: interviewee
text: But there was no such a thing as a major world get-together of ex-servicemen. And the only time I saw anything of the kind was the film from Bergen-Belsen, which you probably saw on television. It was the first that the British have released, after 50 years in the museum, a film which could be called of some sort of a proof. But it's a film. It's still a film. It's not, not the living people themselves who physically liberated those camps, which would have been necessary for it. And it would never have-- would have put in, would have content into history as something which actually never could be erased, that even that possibility it never happened. And I thought to myself, the one and only thing I can do after all these years, to give my own testimony, to give my own story like others did, whether the world will believe it or not. I don't know. Because, because all the, all the proof so far, it's only a matter of time until they rip it to pieces and say it was put on. It was-- the interviewers were paid. The, the, the people who were interviewed were put to it, propaganda, whatever. Unfortunately, this is, this is, we have to face it. But nevertheless, this is all, after 50 years, I can do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Gardos, on behalf of the Shoah Foundation, I'd like to thank you very much for sharing your testimony with us today.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much for taking the effort and the time. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I, I would just like to commend you of what you're doing. Because it's not, it's not a job what you're doing. I think it's far, far more important. And people like yourself, I think really, really deserve [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to be commended on-- and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] oh, I don't know what sort of reward. Because after all the-- you know, you must go through dozens, hundreds of people. And you still have the patience and the time to devote to, to, to single individuals with little stories, big-- whatever, you shouldn't call them stories. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Unique stories.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it's not-- I thank you. I am-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this photographs of Mr. Gardos?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a wedding photo of my mother and father.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: And their name is-- my mother's name was Maria. And my father's name was Jeno.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their Hebrew name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Miriam [HEBREW]. And my father's name was Asher [HEBREW] ben [HEBREW].

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year would this photo have been taken?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Ujpest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. How old were you in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I was two or two and a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what year would this have been taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This would have been 1931 or 1932.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was the photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's was taken in, in Bercel, in the  village I was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank-- Who are these photographs of?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left hand side is my grandfather, from my, my mother's father, with my two little nephews, who perished in, in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their name is-- in English or in Hungarian?

speaker: interviewer
text: Both.

speaker: interviewee
text: Both. The little boy is Jansci, which is Johnny. And the little girl is Susan.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Last name is Gardos.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the gentleman on the left, his full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His full name is Joseph [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is taken in, in Ujpest in one of, one of the paths of the factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year, about?

speaker: interviewee
text: That, that, yes, that would have been 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's the photograph of the people on the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, my grandfather and my grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: And her name is Janka. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the next is my uncle, their son. His name is Bela, Bela Gardos.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his fate?

speaker: interviewee
text: He, he is the one who was shot in the last days of the war, in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken also somewhere in 1942, 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: In?

speaker: interviewee
text: That, in one of the-- the place is called [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Balatonlelle. I'm sorry, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this photograph of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my great uncle and my great aunt. Their name is Gross, [NON-ENGLISH] Gross, and Samuel Gross.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was around 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Ujpest, in their, at their house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this photograph of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it is-- it's hard to believe, but it's me.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in 1950.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Adelaide, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. This is your wedding photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it is, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What date were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was on the 14th of April, 1957.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was his photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Sydney, at the, at the Temple Emanuel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, sitting down, and my wife in the background, holding Robby, and Julie next to her. Robby my son and Julie my daughter of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was that taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was 19-- 1967, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the photo was taken in Sydney?

speaker: interviewee
text: The photo was taken in Sydney, yes, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this photograph of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my three children, Julie, Robby, and-- actually, sorry, I have to retract. It, it is Julie, my daughter, Danny, my youngest son, and my nephew, my sister's son, the little one on Julie's lap. Danny's a Down syndrome child. He's 26 now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was the photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That, that would have been taken somewhere in 19-- 1980.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Sydney?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Sydney, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thanks.
